data.title: The Only Dividend Strategy I'd Trust In A 3.5% Fed Funds World
data.description:
When investing in high-yielding stocks, one of the biggest considerations to keep in mind is the direction of interest rates moving forward. This is because many high-yield stocks are bond proxies, such as REITs (VNQ) like Realty Income (O) or preferred ETFs like the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), or even infrastructure stocks like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC). When interest rates fall, these investments tend to soar higher, and when interest rates rise, they tend to slump.

Meanwhile, business development companies (BDCs) (BIZD) are often the opposite. Whenever the Fed enters a rate-hiking cycle, BDCs tend to move higher, whereas when the Fed cuts rates, BDCs tend to experience dividend cuts and see their stocks fall out of favor because they invest in floating-rate loans primarily. However, my personal view and that of the market at the moment is that interest rates are likely to remain in a fairly narrow band for the foreseeable future. In this article, I will detail why and share how I am investing as a result.

Rates Likely Staying Put

The Federal Reserve has held the Fed funds rate steady at between 3.5% and 3.75% for two consecutive meetings to start 2026. The dot plot and CME's FedWatch currently project between zero and one rate cuts as the most likely scenario this year, with perhaps one more in 2027. This means that for the foreseeable future, the market expects the federal funds rate to remain around 3.5%.

What this means is that investors who are betting heavily on capital appreciation from falling interest rates by piling into REITs, utilities (XLU), preferreds (PFF), and other bond proxies are likely to be disappointed. At the same time, investors in sectors like BDCs who are hoping for a rebound in net investment income from rising rates are also likely to be disappointed. Thus, in this sort of environment, value investing and opportunistic capital recycling are going to be increasingly important for driving total returns rather than simply making a successful macro call on where interest rates are headed.

My Core Investment Strategy

So, how am I investing with this in mind? First of all, my core fundamental approach to investing remains the same. In fact, I'm putting even more emphasis on it now, namely buying high-quality businesses that pay high yields with a margin of safety built into the valuation across seven core sectors:

  • Midstream MLPs (AMLP)

  • BDCs

  • REITs

  • Alternative asset managers

  • Infrastructure

  • Energy producers (XLE)

  • Precious metals-related investments (GDX)

Beyond that, I'm also finding opportunities in industrials (XLI) and consumer goods stocks, and even a few technology stocks have emerged that pay out high yields. Once I build out my well-diversified portfolio of quality dividend payers on attractive valuations, I then sit back patiently and collect dividend income while I wait for Mr. Market to recognize the embedded value in my investments. Once he begins to wake up to those, I trim and potentially even sell those positions as they approach fair value and recycle the capital into even more attractive opportunities, to not only potentially accelerate the total return compounding process, but also to accelerate dividend growth in my portfolio, as I tend to recycle the capital into other positions that pay out even higher dividend yields since they also trade at cheaper valuations. In fact, it is this very strategy that has enabled me to deliver total return outperformance and, at the same time, generate a 7–8% annualized yield in my portfolios since launching them a little over five years ago.

This strategy is one that I expect to work especially well in a stable rate environment because valuations will be even easier to judge as being too high, just right, or too low. One of the chief uncertainties in setting an accurate valuation, namely, interest rates, should be more stable. The reason why this strategy works so well is that passively managed ETFs like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and, to a lesser extent, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) generally engage in systematic rebalancing, typically once a year. While this does work well in driving dividend growth for SCHD based on how it's structured, the once-a-year programmatic rebalancing is not as opportunistic as my approach. It is also rule-based and therefore unable to account for nuances that an active investor like me can take into account.

Midstream MLPs: Rate-Independent Compounders

So, with this in view, how am I investing in each of the seven core sectors that I mentioned previously?

Midstream MLPs are relatively rate-independent because they generate very stable, inflation-like cash flows. This means that when inflation is elevated, which generally leads to rising rates, their cash flows often increase due to a combination of rising energy prices (since energy is a fairly good inflation hedge) and their inflation-linked contracts also tending to boost their cash flows. Meanwhile, when rates fall with inflation being low, sure, energy prices often drop, as do their contract escalators, but the bond-like nature of their contracted cash flow generally offsets that in terms of the market's perception of the intrinsic value of the asset.

Therefore, my approach here is the same as always, and while the surge in energy prices to start the year has led to a strong rally in the midstream space, I'm still finding some attractive opportunities, including names like Energy Transfer (ET) and Western Midstream Partners (WES), as I recently detailed here.

BDCs: A Sweet Spot Emerging

In the BDC space, I think we are about to see a net investment income per share stabilization because spreads, which had been compressing, seem to have bottomed due to capital slowing into the space amid concerns about private credit, as well as the fact that the Fed is likely to pause cutting rates for a while, which means that the floating-rate loans will not see their base rates decline further for a while either.

This creates a sweet spot for BDC income, since base rates are still fairly high relative to where they have been over much of private credit's history, and the economy remains in sound shape, especially for senior secured loans. When you combine that with the very attractive valuations available in the space in blue-chip names like Hercules Capital Corporation (HTGC), Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and others, now is a great time to put money to work in the BDC space, in my view, especially given that non-accrual rates remain quite low.

REITs: Selective Opportunities in Multi-Family

Meanwhile, in the REIT sector, you have to be really selective because REITs tend to only experience dramatic upside in most cases when interest rates shift dynamically, as they generally do not grow very much, and the ones that do grow a lot tend not to yield very much. Therefore, I'm being very selective and looking for blue-chip names that are materially undervalued relative to their private market value, have stable cash flows, attractive dividend yields, and potential upside catalysts.

One place where I'm seeing a lot of this is in the multi-family space with names like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), Camden Property Trust (CPT), and several others. I expect improving supply and demand dynamics, especially in some Sun Belt markets, in the coming years to boost these REITs materially higher as they close their discounts to private market values, pay out dividends with ~5% yields, and grow at a solid clip as well.

Alternative Asset Managers and Infrastructure

Meanwhile, in the alternative asset management space, picks are plentiful, including Blackstone (BX), Ares Management (ARES), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), and several others. I recently released a deep dive into why I think OWL is a particularly attractive buy right now. However, all these names look very undervalued to me, since they're being put in the penalty box for their private credit exposure, even though their underlying private credit fundamentals are solid and their long-term growth outlooks are very attractive, especially given that they're diversified into areas like real estate and AI infrastructure that are both experiencing solid growth right now.

They pay attractive dividends, have strong balance sheets, and asset-light business models. In the infrastructure space, the Brookfield subsidiaries, like BIP and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), as well as well-run, actively managed CEFs like the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), are attractive ways to play AI infrastructure alongside utilities while generating attractive mid- to high single-digit yields along with growth over time. This way, we are not overly dependent on interest rates falling precipitously to drive double-digit annualized total returns.

Energy Producers: Still Finding Value

The energy production segment is hard to find attractive values right now, given that they have rallied so strongly in the wake of the Iran war. Compounders like Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), Exxon Mobil (XOM), and Chevron (CVX) should be quality picks over the long term, and more energy-sensitive midstream infrastructure names like ONEOK (OKE) could also potentially fit in this basket, especially after its recent dip.

You could also play the inverse of it by betting on industrial stocks that tend to suffer from high energy prices, like United Parcel Service (UPS), as this one should soar higher if energy prices ever move lower. You could combine it with a blue-chip energy producer like CNQ to hedge against energy prices in either direction, all while generating attractive yields and solid long-term growth potential.

Why Precious Metals Leases Beat Mining Stocks

Finally, in the precious metals space (GLD)(SLV), I like investing primarily in gold and silver leases because you own bullion fully, do not pay any management fees, and generate a mid-single-digit yield paid monthly. There are also some mining stock names that look attractive at times, including the likes of Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Newmont (NEM), and Barrick Mining (B). However, with these, you have to be picky, and I tend to wait until they sell off pretty aggressively before buying them and view them as trading instruments, whereas my core leasing position is my long-term buy-and-hold precious metals income-generating position.

The Capital Recycling Engine That's Quietly Supercharging My Returns

Within these sectors, I employ opportunistic capital recycling by waiting for picks to come into favor and selling them as they approach fair value, and recycling the capital into other opportunities. Of course, this often is driven by entire sectors moving into favor, as midstream has recently, and gold miners did not too long ago. When that happens, while I may significantly reduce my exposure to a sector, I am always careful to still maintain some exposure in order to maintain adequate portfolio diversification. I then tend to reallocate the proceeds from those sales into a diversified basket of positions, either currently in my portfolio or new ideas that have come along, spread across several sectors to maintain good diversification.

Originally, precious metals were my biggest position by sector; then, when they soared and came into favor last year, I started rotating capital into midstream and energy producers, and that became my biggest position this year. With them soaring higher, I've been gradually reducing my exposure to MLPs and energy stocks and have been pushing the capital into sectors like BDCs, alternative asset managers, and REITs, while still maintaining core positions in energy and precious metals. In fact, energy and precious metals remain my two largest positions, and I also still have some exposure to infrastructure.

Ultimately, no strategy is perfect, and I've gotten plenty of individual and sector names wrong; however, I've been able to get them right far more than wrong. My diversification has helped to keep any of my losers from having too outsized an impact on my portfolio. As a result, I've been able to enjoy significant outperformance over time, and I'm confident I remain positioned to do well, all while generating a high single-digit yield in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Opera: AI-Driven Advertising Prospects - Upside Potential And Rich Dividend Yields
data.description:
I previously reiterated my Buy rating for Opera Limited (NASDAQ:OPRA) in December 2025, given the attractive risk/reward profile from the discounted valuations and the rich forward dividends.

Given the ongoing stock price consolidation, OPRA remains a Great Buy here, aided by the management's promising FQ1'26/FY2026 guidance, the robust return prospects across double digits capital appreciation, inflated hedged dividend yields, and expanded shareholder equities from the new $300M share repurchase program.

OPRA Offers Diversified, Profitable Growth Prospects

Since my last Buy rating, OPRA has offered an excellent stock price return of +9.1% compared to the wider market at +0.7%, thanks to the well received FQ4'25 earnings call in late February 2026.

Combined with the rich dividends, the stock has delivered an even richer total return of +12.1%, with it underscoring their compelling dual pronged return prospects as discussed in my last article.

1. FQ1'26 Preview

For now, with OPRA set to report their FQ1'26 performance in April 23, 2026, I urge readers to compare their upcoming performance against the management's previously offered revenue guidance at $170.5M at the midpoint (+19.4% YoY) and adj EBITDA at $39M (+20.7% YoY).

Despite these promising numbers, I want to offer another metric for consideration, ie: their adj EPS numbers, since their recent performance has been rather underwhelming.

For example, OPRA has suffered through:

  • the elevated cost of inventory sold (the cost for third party advertising inventory that is sold to our customers along with our own inventory to better serve our advertisers’ demand) at $205.12M in FY2025 (+72.8% YoY) against
  • the slower growing advertising revenues of $396.04M (+36.2% YoY),
  • with it contributing to the deteriorating gross margins of 64.1% (-8.3 points YoY).

With the management expecting to drive further growth in the Opera Ads platform, I am of the opinion that OPRA's upcoming FQ1'26 adj EPS performance is likely to underwhelm expectations, as similarly observed in the last two bottom-line misses in FQ3'25 and FQ4'25.

Combined with the stagnant adj EBITDA margins at ~23%/24% since FY2023 and the flat guidance entering FY2026, I urge readers to temper their expectations entering the FQ1'26 earnings call.

Otherwise, despite the growth pains from the higher advertising costs, OPRA's robust revenue growth and stable profit margins have contributed to the consensus' promising forward estimates, with the company expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.2%/+23.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a CAGR of +10.7%/-0.9% between FY2019 levels and FY2025 levels, respectively, with it apparent that the recent stock price underperformance is unwarranted.

2. Robust Advertising/Browser Prospects In An AI World

Beyond the advertising segment's near-term dilutive impact on the bottom-lines, it is apparent that OPRA has been able to rapidly monetize their new e-commerce advertising opportunities, as observed in the high double digits revenue growth in FY2025 and further expansion across the $100B of e-commerce advertising TAM.

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the growing advertiser partnerships and the higher average spend per advertiser by +56% YoY in FY2025 (for the top 50 advertiser cohort), with it mitigating their stagnant Monthly Active Users [MAU] in the Western regions and in the GX gaming browser.

Particularly, OPRA has demonstrated their ability to directly monetize Opera AI in its browser, as observed in the "200% growth in non-search query revenue," thanks to the direct promotion of its advertising partner "either as a part of the URL experience or in an AI test with Opera AI."

As the management reports further adoption trends of Opera AI in its browsers across its users/advertising partners, it is unsurprising that the management has hinted at the non-search query segment to be their " fastest-growing revenue component in 2026."

Readers may also want to closely monitor the management's commentaries for the Opera Neon's subscription trends, since the Agentic AI browser's public launch on December 11, 2025 may be fully accretive from FQ1'26 onwards.

Moving beyond the browser segment, OPRA is seemingly tapping into the increasingly crowded fintech sector through the prior launch of MiniPay, a stablecoin wallet, embedded into its browser.

This is aided by MiniPay's integration with numerous large partners, including Mercado Libre (MELI) in Latam and numerous banks in the US/the EU.

With the decentralized/expanded fintech approach (paying bills/top up data/P2P remittance, amongst others) already contributing to the higher adoption trends at 13M activated wallets and 390M transactions, as numerous governments also embrace stable coins/cryptocurrencies, it is unsurprising that the management has hinted at their global fintech ambitions ahead.

These underscore OPRA's well diversified monetization capabilities across e-commerce advertising, AI-driven affiliate queries, and even, fintech offerings, with it underscoring their likely to be robust growth prospects beyond the free to download browser and the nascent Neon subscription trends.

3. Compellingly Valued, Profitable Growth Trends

Thanks to the correction from the prior 2025 highs and the YTD consolidation, I am of the opinion that OPRA's bottom-line headwinds have already been baked into its discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 10.90x, down from the 5Y mean of 19.14x and the sector median of 22.31x.

Based on the already discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 10.90x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +23.5%, it is apparent that OPRA remains attractively valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.46x, compared to the wider market at 1.38x.

As a browser platform, OPRA continues to boast a rich FY2025 Rule of 43.7% outperformance as well (+8 points YoY), across FY2025 revenue growth by +27.9% YoY (compared to a year ago at +21.1% YoY) and the FY2025 Free Cash Flow margins of 15.8% (+1.2 points from FY2024 levels of 14.6%).

When comparing OPRA's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.68x and the FY2025 Rule of 43.7% outperformance to its direct browser peers, including:

  • diversified advertising/hyperscaler - Chrome from Alphabet (GOOG) at 8.03x/33.1% (FY2025 revenue growth by +15% YoY and FCF margins of 18.1%) and
  • Enterprise SaaS/hyperscaler - Bing from Microsoft (MSFT) at 8.55x/42% (LTM revenue growth by +16.7% sequentially and FCF margins of 25.3%),

it is apparent that OPRA's profitable growth trend (revenue growth and FCF margins) is cheaply valued here, with it offering interested investors an excellent margin of safety.

Most importantly, OPRA's growth prospects are likely to be resilient, as observed in their still rich FCF generation despite the prior data center investment and the healthy net cash to adj EBITDA ratio of 1.09x in FY2025, compared to GOOG at 0.53x and MSFT at 0.42x.

These underscore OPRA's ability to "suffer" the near-term gross margin and adj EPS headwinds, before they eventually achieve improved operating scale over the next few years.

OPRA Remains A Great Buy Here

For now, OPRA has continued to trade sideways since 2023, with it underperforming my prior expectations albeit with the stock also increasingly becoming cheaper in P/E valuations as their bottom-lines grow.

These reasons are also why the stock remains extremely compelling as it trades along the P/E of ~10x since October 2025, with the gap to the sector P/E median of 22.31x and the peer group mean of ~20x along with the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $2.11 implying its rich upside potential to:

  • my base-case long-term price target of $21.10, or
  • my bull-case long-term price target of $42.20 (P/E of 20x).

Combined with the established annual dividend payouts of $0.80 per share, there appears to be an excellent:

  • base-case total return prospect at approximately $8.17, or the equivalent +53%, or
  • bull-case total return prospect at approximately $29.27, or the equivalent +190%,

from OPRA's current share prices of $15.33.

Readers must also note the tailwinds from OPRA's new $300M in share authorization, with the reduced share count likely to be adj EPS/dividend per share accretive in the long-run.

Pending that speculative upward re-rating in valuations, patient investors may enjoy OPRA's rich dividend yields of 5.22% compared to the sector median of 1.42%, with it presenting an excellent inflation hedge investment thesis compared to the global inflation average of 4.2% in 2025 and ~4.3% over the past ten years.

As a result of the richer advertising monetization trends along with the discounted valuations/the rich dividend yields from the prior sideways trading, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the OPRA stock here.

data.title: RenaissanceRe: Preferred Stock Hasn't Been This Appealing In Years
data.description:
Introduction

As it has been approximately 2 years since I last had a look at RenaissanceRe (RNR) and its preferred shares, I wanted to see if the recently filed annual report still indicates the preferred dividends are well covered. After a few of the securities in my fixed income portion of my portfolio have been called, I now have the bandwidth to add new positions provided the risk reward ratio is acceptable.

The financial results confirm the preferred dividend is well-covered

In this update, I will have a look at the financial performance of the company to see how well the preferred dividends are covered. I also wanted to see if there is any realistic balance sheet risk involved with the investment in a senior security issued by RenaissanceRe.

As the income statement below shows, the reinsurance company reported a total gross amount of premiums written of approximately $11.7B (stable versus the preceding year) which resulted in approximately $9.9B in net premiums earned. While that is lower than in the preceding year, Renaissance’s financial results were boosted by a higher net investment income mainly due to a $1.18 billion realized and unrealized gain on its investment portfolio.

This compensated the higher amount of net claims and claim expenses, as well as some of the other operating expenses like the acquisition expenses. Meanwhile the corporate expenses and operational expenses actually decreased during 2025.

All these elements ultimately resulted in a net profit of approximately $3.62B, of which $935M was attributable to non-controlling interests. And of the $2.68B that was attributable to RenaissanceRe, only $35.4M was required to cover the preferred dividends.

This indicates RenaissanceRe can very easily cover all preferred dividends considering the company needed less than 1.5% of its attributable net income to ensure all preferred dividends are correctly paid. And that is not because the company got lucky in 2025 as even in 2024 less than 2% of the net income was required to cover the preferred dividends.

A second important element I'm keeping close tabs on is to see how much common equity is on the balance sheet. Common equity ranks junior to any preferred securities and should be seen as a cushion to absorb the first losses in case of adverse events.

As you can see in the image above, the total amount of equity attributable to RenaissanceRe is $11.6B, of which just $750M consists of preferred equity. This indeed means that there's almost $11 billion in common equity that ranks junior to the preferred securities. And just like the strong preferred dividend coverage ratio it goes without saying that having this much common equity on the balance sheet increases the safety level of the preferred stock.

The 6.7% preferred dividend yield is getting interesting

RenaissanceRe Currently has two issues of preferred securities outstanding and I think combination of both makes the most sense. While I look at fixed income from a long duration point of view because I want to secure cash flows for years to come, the recent strong results could increase the company's appetite to call the more expensive capital.

The F-shares (RNR.PR.F) have a 5.75% preferred dividend yield and are callable at any time. The current share price of the Series F preferred shares is $21.20, resulting in a preferred dividend yield of approximately 6.75%.

The Series G shares (RNR.PR.G) have a lower dividend yield of 4.20% ($1.05 per share per year, paid in four quarterly installments of $0.2625 per quarter). They can be called from July 2026 on at the principal value of $25.00, but they can also be called before if a regulatory capital event occurs (at no premium) or if a rating agency event occurs (at a 2% premium). Considering a 4.2% coupon guarantees a lower cost of capital, the series F preferred stock will likely be called before series G will be retired. The current yield is quite similar so the choice between both series really depends on the preference to avoid a potential call.

Investment thesis

While I predominantly focus on the preferred stock issued by RenaissanceRe, I also keep an eye on the common shares given the reinsurance company's strong track records of growing its book value and its tangible book value per share. At the end of last year the total book value per share came in at $247 while the tangible book value jumped sharply to $230 per share. This means the current premium of approximately 30% to the tangible book value is a very acceptable ratio given the robust track record of the company.

That's also why I think that the preferred stock - despite being non-cumulative - offers an attractive risk reward ratio given the 6.7% yield thanks to the strong dividend coverage and asset coverage ratios. I currently have no position in any of the preferred securities issued by Renaissance, but I will likely initiate a long position in the next few weeks.

data.title: Q1 Earnings Kick Off With Major Banks' Results: Bank of America, Netflix In Focus
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages struggled to stay higher on Friday as the core retail inflation print for March came in slightly cooler than expected, while investors continued to brace for U.S.-Iran war tensions. The March U.S. core Consumer Price Index, which excludes food and energy, came in weaker at +0.2% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus. In addition, the Consumer Sentiment Index came in lower at 47.6 for April vs. the 52.0 consensus, according to preliminary data by the University of Michigan Survey.

As the first-quarter earnings season kicks off next week, with major banks reporting their results, only a few economic data are scheduled. Existing home sales data for March will be released on Monday, with PPI data scheduled for Tuesday. Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing numbers are due on Thursday along with the jobless claims data.

Goldman Sachs (GS), JPMorgan (JPM), Citigroup (C), Bank of America (BAC), and Netflix (NFLX) are among the companies reporting their results next week.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday: Goldman Sachs. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: JPMorgan, J&J (JNJ), Wells Fargo (WFC), Citigroup. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: ASML (ASML), Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Netflix, PepsiCo (PEP). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Coherent (COHR) and AMD (AMD) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Service Mega Fortune (MGRT), Avis Budget (CAR), and Tilly's (TLYS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include MDxHealth SA (MDXH), PepGen (PEPG), and Byrna Technologies (BYRN).

IPO watch: Encore Medial (EMI), Madison Air Solutions (MAIR), and Arxis (ARXS) are expected to price their IPOs next week and begin to trade. The quiet period ends on Janus Living (JAN) and Guardian Metals Resources (GMR) to free up analysts to post ratings. The countdown for investors to see SpaceX's (SPACE) financials ahead of a June IPO continues.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Abbott Labs (ABT), AbbVie (ABBV), Johnson Outdoors (JOUT), and Acuity Brands (AYI).

Bank of America Earnings Preview: Bank of America is expected to report first-quarter earnings of $1.01 on revenue of $29.95B, with the Q1 print being seen as a test for the bank's resilience to macro headwinds, with potential downside to profitability and loan growth. The company had guided 2026 NII in the range of 5%-7%; however, it now faces risks due to a flatter yield curve and diminished rate cut expectations, analysts said. Hence, the management's annual NII guidance will be keenly watched for. Meanwhile, with consumer confidence at a dangerously low level, consumer loan growth remains fragile. Some relief may be expected if commercial loan growth continues to do the heavy-lifting like the previous year.

Netflix Earnings Preview: Netflix's consensus EPS estimate for the first quarter is $0.77, while revenue is projected at $12.17B. As the Warner Bros acquisition saga has now come to an end, analysts believe the focus this quarter has shifted back to the streaming giants' core growth areas- advertising and its content & engagement. Advertising revenue will also depend on how much progress the firm has made in expanding live sports on its platform. Moreover, the company again raised its subscription prices last month. While the impact of the new pricing structure is not expected to be felt at least until the second quarter, analysts believe, it will be vital to see where the company plans to invest the capital, as this will signal the trajectory of its ads business.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bitmine Immersion Q2 Preview: Ethereum Thesis Facing Important Report Card
data.description:
Setting The Stage

About a year ago, Bitmine Immersion Technologies, Inc. (BMNR) was what I would describe as a small-cap Bitcoin miner. It was operationally unremarkable among a crowded field of crypto mining companies. However, the narrative is different today, with it being the largest Ethereum treasury in the world and the second largest corporate crypto treasury globally behind Strategy Inc. (MSTR) and freshly listed on the New York Stock Exchange after graduating from NYSE America on April 9.

This significant transformation, which happened in under 9 months, to me is either a masterclass in strategic pivoting or maybe the most ambitious bet ever by a publicly traded company on a single digital asset. As I hold this in mind, the Q2 2026 earnings report scheduled for April 15 will be the first sustained look investors will get at whether the financial architecture underneath the headline numbers holds together. This makes this upcoming quarterly report the most important one in the last couple of years, if not ever in the history of the company so far.

Its “Alchemy of 5%” framework, stating a goal of acquiring 5% of all Ethereum tokens in circulation, has defined its entire capital allocation strategy. As of April 5th, 2026, Bitmine was holding 4,803,334 ETH at about $2,123 per token, reaching 79% of its target. Tom Lee (Chairman) has positioned ETH as a “wartime store of value,” noting it has outperformed the S&P 500 by 1,130 basis points since the onset of the US and Iran conflict and has beaten gold by 1,840 basis points over the same time frame.

Estimates & Outlook

As we head to Q2 2026, the consensus revenue estimates at Seeking Alpha stand at $18.40 million, up from $1.52 million in Q2 2025. This marks a continuation of the strong recent upward momentum in revenue, which I expect to be amplified by the recent restructuring highlighted above and which I will get deeper into shortly.

The EPS estimate for Q2 is up $0.17 from -$0.60 in Q2 2025, a signal of the significant impact the company’s new market positioning has on its financials. I look at these against the FY 2026 revenue and EPS estimates of $146.90 million, a 2,310.17% YoY, and $0.69, respectively. But how should investors read these estimates? I think they should do it with calibrated skepticism. In my view, the revenue projection for FY 2026 is almost entirely dependent on MAVAN staking income being recognized as operating revenue, which is a classification that has not yet been formally confirmed in audited financials. With this in mind, I believe the upcoming quarterly report is where the goalposts will get set, as I expect updates on this key aspect.

My take here is that the consensus EPS estimate of $0.69 for 2026 is probably too optimistic on timing but structurally reasonable. If MAVAN’s $196 million in current annualized staking revenue is recognized as income (and there is no obvious reason it would not be, given staking is an active protocol-level service), the forward revenue story becomes highly credible. Having said that, the key variable is dilution with ongoing share issuance to fund ETH purchases. The per share earnings power could be materially eroded even if the headline revenue scales. Investors should track the diluted share count as carefully as the revenue line, especially given the recently announced buyback plan.

Key Focus Areas In The Q2 2026 Report

If you ask me, the single most critical variable is how this company classifies and reports MAVAN staking revenue in its audited financials. With 3,334,637 ETH staked at 2.78% annualized yield, the implied revenue is about $196 million per year. This is more than 30x the total 2025 reported revenue of $6.1 million. It then follows that if this is classified as operating income, it can fundamentally re-rate the company. Should it be deferred, capitalized, or presented only as unrealized appreciation? The estimated revenue and forward PS of 65.27x in 2026 collapses with a revenue likely to slump to the annualized region of below $10 million operating reality currently.

Another key area of focus is the balance sheet and buyback. In early April, BMNR held $864 million in cash alongside 4.8 million ETH and no net debt. To me, this is a fortress balance sheet for a company of this age.

I am looking at this against its share repurchase initiative, expanded from $1 billion authorization to $4 billion announced with the NYSE uplisting, ranked among the largest buybacks of 2026. The management’s clarity on how this will be deployed without impairing the ETH accumulation strategy is an important update worth watching.

The Bull Take: From Treasury To Infrastructure

By now I think it's clear to anyone that the most compelling long term argument for this company is its MAVAN transformation from a passive crypto treasury into an income generating infrastructure. At its full deployment capacity of about 4.8 million ETH at the current 2.78% staking yield, annualized rewards reach about $282 million. If ETH recovers, each 1% price move adds roughly $100 million to the balance sheet. Assuming MAVAN scales as expected, the BMNR could be generating significant recurring cash flow within 12-18 months.

Counterargument: Valuation & Execution Risk

Given its share price of approximately $12 and a market cap just below $10 billion, BMNR trades at 0.85x crypto NAV.

This valuation ignores two uncomfortable realities. First off, the $7.2 million in trailing revenue cannot cover its trailing operating expenses of $236.5 without continued dilutive issuance or debt. Secondly, the executive compensation amendments filed early this month, including a $1.75 million annual incentive for the CFO vesting in one year, raise legitimate alignment concerns.

Conclusion

In my view, Bitmine is not a stock for the investors who require traditional earnings metrics to establish conviction. Rather, it is a macro bet on Ethereum dressed in the clothes of a public company. The upcoming quarterly report on April 15th, 2026, is where the bet gets stress tested by auditors, analysts, and investors now operating under NYSE level scrutiny.

The revenue forecast hinges almost entirely on MAVAN staking income being formally recognized. It is a possible outcome that requires clarity from management to be actionable. Given this background, I rate the stock a hold as we await clarity on the major growth driver here.

data.title: I Am Sharing Two Of My Retirement High-Yield Gems
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Growing up, we had a small treehouse in our backyard. It wasn’t a fancy one. It was just a wooden platform with a railing, a ladder leading to it, and a roof on top. Whenever I felt low, whether it was due to getting a scolding or something else that didn’t go my way, I’d climb up that tree house and sit there for hours, listening to the rustling leaves, and the chirping birds and insects. It provided a good view of the neighborhood. I could see the postman make his rounds, and people walking their dogs. Some activities were routine, while others were seasonal.

Things felt calmer from that spot. Whatever was bothering me didn’t disappear, but it didn't bother me as much. Looking back, I think that treehouse just gave me a chance to clear my head and reset. Rough moments usually pass if you give them time.

As we grow up, we find other comfort objects or activities to take the stress out. Whether it is cooking, playing a few rounds of golf, or listening to music, most of us have a temporary escape from the daily demands of life.

In our financial lives, income investing plays a very similar role to that tree house. When markets get choppy and headlines scream for our attention, we all need our own version of that tree house. Something steady, something safe, something that rewards patience instead of panic.

That’s exactly what the two +7% retirement income gems in this article are built to do. They pay you to stay calm, no matter how noisy the world below gets. Let’s dive in.

Pick #1: UTF – Yield 7.4%

Tech companies are busy competing to demonstrate that they have the most powerful models and solutions. They are sparing no expense towards building infrastructure, acquiring talent, and building software. Even non-tech companies are eager to demonstrate to their customers that they can also party with the cool kids and win with AI.

Competition is good for consumers. When competition is so aggressive, customers are the biggest beneficiaries. Remember how a few years ago, every broker out there eliminated commissions to compete with Robinhood (HOOD)? In the months ahead, we will get to see and experience innovative AI solutions that do things people only dreamed of just years ago.

Competition is also good for those who provide services to the big spenders (innovators). In the AI race, there are many service providers, and utilities are one of the most significant.

Entergy Louisiana (ENR) recently announced an agreement with Meta to support a hyperscale data center in Northeast Louisiana. META will pay the full cost of service, including CapEx to build the infrastructure and operating expenses for 20 years. Several other utility companies across the U.S. have signed long-term power purchase agreements with tech giants, providing growth CapEx and steady revenues. The U.S. has an aging power infrastructure; Big Tech will be paying heavily to modernize it (to a certain extent), and utility firms will reap long-term rewards.

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) is a CEF (closed-end fund) that manages $4.3 billion in total assets. UTF has 301 holdings, with 57% of its portfolio comprising electric utility, gas distribution, and midstream firms. UTF’s top ten holdings represent 28% of its invested assets, and happen to be some of the most prominent energy and utility companies in North America. Source

UTF also holds corporate bonds and preferred shares, which further increase the fund’s cash flow predictability. UTF delivers monthly dividends, which it earns through collected dividends and capital gains through active management of holdings.

As of December 31, 2025, UTF reported $220.8 million in unrealized gains, which exceeds what the fund paid out in distributions for FY 2025. Over the past decade, UTF has delivered steady NAV growth, while maintaining steady monthly payouts to shareholders.

Not surprisingly, the CEF recently raised its monthly distribution by 6.5% to $0.165/share, which calculates to a 7.6% forward yield.

UTF presents an excellent way to tap into the utility sector, one with predictable cash flows from a highly inelastic sector.

Pick #2: GNL Preferreds – Up To 8.1% Yields

Global Net Lease (GNL) is an internally managed REIT that manages a portfolio of single-tenant net-lease assets in the U.S., Canada, and Europe. GNL’s primary focus is industrial and commercial properties, with modest exposure to office properties. Source

GNL ended 2025 with a portfolio of 820 properties at 97% occupancy, with a weighted-average remaining lease term of 6.1 years. The REIT’s top ten tenants represent only 29% of straight-line rent, with no single tenant accounting for more than 5.6% of the SLR (this being FedEx). About 66% of GNL’s SLR comes from investment-grade tenants.

Even GNL’s small office property footprint is fundamentally healthy, with top tenants being largely investment-grade, like ING, Deutsche Bank, AT&T, and the U.S. General Services Administration. Roughly 80% of GNL’s office portfolio is leased to investment-grade tenants, with 87% of the leases carrying contractual rent escalation.

2025 has been a transformational year for GNL, with full-year AFFO/share being $0.99, exceeding the REIT’s guidance range. GNL ended the year with Net Debt to Adj. EBITDA at 6.7x (down from 7.6x), 98% fixed-rate debt, and a weighted-average interest rate of 4.2% (down from 4.8% at the end of 2024). GNL has well-laddered debt maturities, with only $95 million maturing in 2026.

The REIT's liquidity position doubled to $962 million, and its rapidly improving financial health led to Fitch Ratings upgrading its corporate credit rating to investment-grade "BBB-".

Since the beginning of GNL’s property disposition strategy in 2024, the REIT has sold $3.4 billion of assets, which have been instrumental in maintaining its dividend after three cuts in three years. GNL repurchased 17.2 million shares for $136 million in 2025.

GNL’s strong financial performance led to a meaningful rebound in the common stock price, and strong outperformance against the REIT benchmark Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ).

During 2025, GNL spent $43.7 million towards preferred stock and $207 million towards common stock dividends. The common dividend continues to have tight coverage from the $221 million AFFO (which the REIT calculates after the preferred dividends are paid).

  • 7.25% Series A, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.A) – Yield 8%

  • 6.875% Series B, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.B) – Yield 8%

  • 7.50% Series D, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.D) – Yield 8%

  • 7.375% Series E, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.E) – Yield 8.1%

Looking ahead, GNL expects $0.80-0.84/share in AFFO for 2026, and $250-350 million in acquisition and disposition volume, with a focus on disposing of certain office assets and picking up single-tenant retail and industrial properties. Despite improving fundamentals, we will continue to stay away from GNL common stock, and focus on its well-covered perpetual preferreds.

At this time, all GNL preferreds offer ~8% yields at a considerable discount to par. Based on GNL’s financing activity in recent months, it is quite unlikely that they will consider calling these preferreds anytime soon, providing a strong runway for steady yields for the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

GNL’s preferred shares pay steady, contractual dividends backed by the cash flows of the globally diversified net lease REIT. UTF delivers steady monthly income from cash flows from infrastructure and utility companies, which are designed to weather storms while maintaining their profitability.

The High Dividend Opportunities portfolio has many such holdings that quietly pay regardless of the economic outlook. Together, they create a grown-up version of that treehouse.

Volatility doesn’t stop on its own, and headlines won’t quiet down. If you keep responding to these inevitable forces governing the markets, you will not be happy. But with income investments like the ones discussed in this article, you can let the noise fade into the background.

Get paid to stay calm. Sometimes, that’s all the comfort you need.

data.title: The Software Narrative Is Leaking Badly Again Thanks To Anthropic Mythos
data.description:
Anthropic Mythos Causes Panic In Software

Software stocks are crumbling again, as Anthropic (ANTHRO) strikes with yet another more and more powerful release, this time, the mythical Mythos.

The yet to be publicly released AI model has sent shockwaves through SaaS (IGV) after what was a brief respite following the shivers delivered back in February.

Back then, Wall Street came to the defense of SaaS, arguing that such a widespread destruction in the bullish narrative doesn’t have solid fundamental foundations.

But, in an environment that’s increasingly shrouded with AI model-induced uncertainties, I guess it was always going to be a tough call in nailing the absolute bottom in the already well-battered software plays.

The aura of invincibility behind Anthropic’s latest AI model has also shaken up Wall Street executives, regulators, and even the US government.

It is making us wonder whether these AI models have reached such a phenomenal scale, intelligence, and capabilities that, perhaps, they may no longer be safe for general availability.

That they are capable of posing substantive risks to national security, governments, infrastructure, and enterprise security on a massive scale that simply has no precedent.

At least according to Anthropic, they need to hold Mythos back to help prevent adversaries from gaining a heads up on their capabilities ahead of the defenders.

Even Cybersecurity Companies Are Also Under Fire

What’s more worrying is that it seems to suggest that even the current gold standard in cybersecurity, such as CrowdStrike (CRWD) or Palo Alto Networks (PANW), may need more time to preview the model and find the required solutions to help defend against these threats.

And if these upcoming AI models (I believe Mythos wouldn’t be the last) do prove to be extremely powerful and capable of finding the previously unknown vulnerabilities and exploiting them in double quick time, our software vendors could be in for more than just a bit of trouble.

No wonder the selloff gained traction this week, as the market digested the alarming news and glaring developments.

If Anthropic doesn’t think the current cybersecurity or enterprise safeguards are sufficient to help thwart these attacks, then the argument that the current software leaders have the necessary perimeter defenses over vibe-coded software might no longer be that meaningful.

Because, it now conflates the narrative that the enterprise software companies have tried to pander to us. That they have the data ecosystem, the security architecture, and the years of experience in the business to deliver reliable value safely to customers.

However, this time, that context is under intense strain.

If Mythos could be that formidable enough to even threaten the best in the business, why should companies entrust their future to these software incumbents?

Could it be possible that companies start to vibe code more of their ecosystem (and let Mythos harden them), instead of relying further on software that may not be that safe after all?

Generally, I think more investors could contend that there’s no safe space in the SaaS world.

Unless we could diagnose these vulnerabilities confidently, the market will think that the investment thesis in software is getting even more fuzzy right now.

Most Leading SaaS Firms Are Deeply Underwater Year-To-Date

No surprises that among the largest software companies by market cap, only one, Fortinet (FTNT), is in the positive zone YTD based on total return.

And most are mired deep in a bear market, corresponding to the widespread selloff as investors scrambled for cover.

There’s simply nowhere to hide, not even in Microsoft (MSFT), not even in Palantir (PLTR).

However, I also think that taking such a sledgehammer approach in deeming all software companies bad appears to be overly harsh.

Cybersecurity companies like Palo Alto Networks and CrowdStrike are obviously best in class, no?

Which is why they are both included in the first 50 or so firms previewing Anthropic’s Mythos.

It’s not known whether the AI company will expand this list to encompass more firms over time, but I do think that general availability appears to be unlikely in the near term.

Make no mistake about it. Project Glasswing is all about software, and Anthropic made it crystal clear with the tagline “securing critical software for the AI era.”

Note that the list also includes Microsoft, informing us just how select this list is.

Will Anthropic Now Become The Kingmaker Of SaaS?

There are a few ways to think about the current conundrum facing Anthropic and also the software industry in general.

Stratechery’s Ben Thompson presented what I think appears to be a possible way to assess the current position and clout that Anthropic possesses.

I’m not about to speculate how Anthropic will align, and what its incentives are.

From what I can observe and assess, it does seem like a prudent move to launch a preview to selected firms, and not make it generally available.

But then, what accords Anthropic the so-called “right” to choose who it wants to select into this preview list?

What is the criteria used to assess these early users of the model (or subsequent more powerful models)?

Will it then perhaps provide these companies with a distinct advantage that could also signal to the market that they may come up with better software security than the rest of the firms who aren’t invited to this exclusive group of early previewers?

I don’t know exactly who the other 40 additional firms in this list yet include, but I guess we will get to know them in the next couple of weeks, as we get more updates from the enterprise world testing Mythos capabilities.

And because of this, I think we could already sense from the table and from Anthropic’s list of early previewers that we could see clear bifurcation in the software trade.

Cybersecurity companies are most definitely in the running as the least disrupted, by virtue of their defensive roles for the enterprise ecosystem.

Yet, it seems like only those companies that have built a consolidated platform like PANW and CRWD could withstand the onslaught better.

But, cybersecurity companies also face immense reputational risks because if these AI models could substantially raise the capabilities of the attackers, then an unintended and significant breach might be all it takes to cause investors to lose confidence.

Yet, Not All SaaS Will Be Disrupted

And it’s common knowledge that cybersecurity companies are also generally much more expensive when compared to the software average (last printed 21.5x in forward earnings multiples).

Even Palantir isn’t immune.

And that's despite posting phenomenal growth rates, having an established consumption-based pricing model (as compared to the mostly seat-based models of some of its peers), while also acting as the orchestration layer, Palantir arguably occupies a treasured position in the pantheon of software greats.

Palantir hasn’t just proven its use cases with the commercial world, but has established firm foundations with government organizations, making it an indispensable partner not only for the government, but also for Anthropic and the other AI companies.

And then we have Microsoft.

Obviously, MSFT’s competencies are so diverse and its monetization strategies so firmly established that I’m not too concerned about whether it could monetize its AI compute capacity.

So Microsoft can make money from AI even as it invests in CapEx to build capacity that should continue to rise. And it has access to OpenAI’s models, while also working closely with Anthropic.

And its decision to double down on higher seat-based pricing indicates that while long-term risks due to seat erosion could hurt the growth, MSFT can counter these effects with higher prices.

The monetization journey is still so early when we think about its current AI Copilot users of 15M as compared to its overall base of users.

So, if there’s one company that has multiple layers in its tech stack to monetize and protect against AI obsolescence, MSFT is a clear standout.

That brings me to the rest of the software companies. I wouldn’t go into detail, as that’s not the objective of this write-up.

But, here’s the essence of my thinking.

Whether these companies operate at the application or systems layer matters. Obviously, if you are at the systems layer, unhinging them should prove to be more challenging than not. That makes sense, right?

For those in the applications layer, if their main customer base is the SMBs, then we should expect these companies to have higher incentives to vibe code their way out of these more pricey software stacks, isn’t it?

For those that act as systems of record, their ability to inject AI into the workflow will become front and center, but it’s not as if Anthropic cannot orchestrate that layer.

The thing is, since the rich collection of data exists within these SaaS companies, they may be adjudged to have the right to price any export of data to these AI models, but we don’t know exactly how these pricing dynamics will work, right?

And since SaaS has always been predicated more on stability, long-duration ARR models that have a more or less predictable runway into the future, the current flux suggests investors are now being forced to rethink whether these assumptions still make any sense at all.

My take is, software companies are obviously under fire.

The market tape should tell you why, as the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF has gone on to take out new lows after the February bottom.

Some of the leading enterprise software firms, like ServiceNow (NOW) and Salesforce (CRM) have also gone on to make new 2026 lows.

Even the cybersecurity gold standards and stalwarts aren’t immune to this maelstrom.

I do think this shakeout hasn’t quite reached its ultimate lows in view of the latest developments as the print breaks down.

But, I do think that after the current selling wave has subsided, we will get to see which firms could come out of the disaster zone less affected than their peers. Let's call this the day after.

Wait patiently for the day after, yeah. This is not over.

data.title: The Muni Market Looks Appealing In Q2
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to discuss the broader macro-backdrop and why this environment may be ripe for municipal (muni) bonds. In my mind the timing couldn't be more relevant because equity markets (and fixed income markets too) have seen an uptick in volatility around the globe. Driven by military conflict and oil supply shocks, we have seen a drawdown in stock prices, and investors are likely looking for some relative safety in the weeks ahead.

There really wasn't much of a place to "hide" in the last month, as this graphic shows us. But bonds did provide some "alpha" in the sense of a smaller price decline. Munis in particular held up slightly better than corporate bonds, and that piqued my interest, of course.

More importantly, I think munis provide a reasonable risk-reward in the coming weeks and months. They have lagged over the past year, but this suggests there is value that others might be missing. This is especially true for income-oriented investors and for those in higher tax brackets because the tax-equivalent yield within this sector is quite attractive. Given that I expect volatility to remain elevated until the Iran conflict is resolved (as much as it could ever be), I believe munis are ripe to buy for the right individual. I will detail the case for why in detail below.

The Income Story Can't Be Ignored

As I noted in the conclusion of the prior section, munis are offering some compelling yields right now. This is especially true if one has a high tax burden. I highlight this because for those in lower tax (or no tax) brackets, then this discussion is a bit more nuanced. You may still like munis, but the yield advantage won't be as pronounced. But if you are sitting with tax exposure in the 30%+ range, then munis are generating an income stream well above corporates and treasuries on a tax-equivalent basis:

It seems to me that those who want "safe" income should be looking at munis. They have a strong historical track record, and the yield advantage is striking.

However, we should consider why this is the case. Munis were a noticeable underperformer in 2025. The price appreciation among corporates (and treasuries) helped push their respective yields down at a faster pace than munis. So while I believe this creates an opportunity for munis, the argument could certainly be made that underperformance for this sector will continue deeper into 2026.

I say this because trends can often persist for a long time even if there is perceived value. Just because I see it doesn't mean the market will. I'm not trying to be contradictory here, but rather to make sure my followers understand the backdrop and why the yield spread exists. The risk of continued weakness for munis is real, and I would never presume to pump an investment idea without highlighting the potential pitfalls.

This Remains An Overlooked Corner Of The Market

Another central point for my broader message to my followers on why I like munis has to do with the fact that many are under-allocated to this sector. Of course, this won't be relevant to every reader, as hopefully there are some out there that have taken my suggestion on munis seriously over the years!

But, in aggregate, US households don't have a lot of muni exposure. This is especially true for those who don't rebalance. As equities post strong returns, their weight amongst the average portfolio keeps climbing. This means even those who have a healthy allocation to munis can see their allocation diminish in terms of total portfolio exposure:

My takeaway is two-fold. One, I think munis are offering some value right now, and that makes me interested regardless. Two, I think many readers can benefit from this reminder because investors remain under-allocated (in my view) as their portfolio percentage of munis continues to fall. As stocks rise, we can get out of whack in terms of balance, and that can make one less diversified than they think. So I will keep highlighting munis as long as this dynamic exists.

State Reserves To Decline, But Remain High

Another positive attribute for munis has to do with state cash reserves. This makes me especially bullish on general obligation (GO) muni bonds, which tend to be backed by the full credit and taxing authority of state and local governments. When reserves are high -as they are now - chances of fiscal strain or default are low. This is good news for investors in the underlying muni securities.

We should recognize that state expenditures are expected to grow in 2026 as federal funding to state and local governments remains on the decline. This is a headwind for muni bonds, to be sure, but the good news is that even if reserves decline as expected, they will remain elevated on a historical basis.

I wanted to highlight this because I don't want my followers to panic over any potential headlines concerning "rising state government spending". While it is important to be aware of state spending levels and monitor the subsequent risks, in aggregate, states are still in a healthy position and handily have the reserves available to make good on their debt obligations. This won't always be the case, of course, if conditions worsen, but that is a discussion for another day as reserves will remain high throughout 2026.

Outlook Still Suggests Lower Rates In The Future

My final bullish point for munis is not necessarily specific to just munis. What I mean is that while this can be a catalyst for higher muni prices in the future, it could also extend to other fixed-income sectors too. I want to illustrate that even if one reads this review and decides munis are not for them, they may find the backdrop warrants increasing exposure to other bond sectors going forward.

The factor here I am referring to is the outlook for interest rates in the US. While the Fed is currently on "pause" with respect to more rate cuts and predictions are getting pushed back a bit, the broader message is still clear to me. This is that rates are going to be lower in the future, not higher, and that means locking-in yields before the next Fed move continues to be the right strategy:

The shift here is there isn't a lot of urgency to get in immediately. Investors have more time at their disposal than it looked like they might when 2026 got underway. The war with Iran and the subsequent rise in oil prices have shifted inflation expectations, which has given the Fed little wiggle room now. But that won't be the case forever.

The market still anticipates interest rates to be lower in 2027 than they are today, and that means investors won't want to wait too long before they capture current yields. I am saying one needs to rush in now. Of course not. But bonds should remain top-of-mind in my view, and I would suggest investors make sure they keep a close eye on rate expectations and not fall behind the curve, so to speak.

Bringing This All Together

So how can we make the most of this opportunity now? I, for one, continue to see value on the long-end of the muni curve. It steepens noticeably after the 10-year duration mark, and this represents a sharp contrast with other fixed-income sectors. This tells me that longer-dated munis continue to offer investors an income stream commensurate with the duration rate risk they would be taking on when buying them:

The good news is that many of the popular ETFs and CEFs that hold muni bonds tend to be longer-term in nature, and that means that one is capturing that duration even with a more passive strategy.

Further, those who have read my work over time know that I love CEFs at discounts. With valuations elevated across the market today, one might surmise there isn't a lot of value to be had in muni-land. But, on the contrary, many funds still exist with high yields due to leverage and double-digit discounts to NAV. This means there are quite a few options to choose from that would fit my criteria:

If funds are not for you, I would contend that with a little bit of research, one could find individual securities that provide a strong income stream without the worry of high expenses that many funds charge. I would advocate buying the bonds from your home state, but diversification never hurts either!

**I own individual muni issues from the state of North Carolina (where I reside). I primarily focus on GO, transportation, and utility bonds.

Bottom-Line

Munis had fallen short in 2025 and saw some weakness over the past month as well. But I think Q2 will see a turning of the tide. The tax-equivalent yield for this sector is very competitive - especially for those of us burdened with a high tax bracket. Further, defaults remain low and state cash reserves remain high. This makes it a relatively safe investment environment. Finally, many investors are under-allocated to munis, and, if we see a change in this regard, leaves significant upside for the sector as demand picks up. For all these reasons, I continue to see value in this corner of the market and suggest to my readers they give the idea some consideration at this time.

data.title: AVIV: Should Keep Rising If The Ceasefire Holds
data.description:
AVIV Overview

The Avantis International Large Cap Value ETF (AVIV) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund with a NAV around $1.2Bn that invests in large-capitalization equity securities outside the U.S. AVIV is a direct equity fund—it holds individual stocks, not other ETFs. The fund's benchmark target is the MSCI World ex USA Value Index, which covers large- and mid-cap value-style companies across 22 developed markets outside the U.S.

The value investment style characteristics for index construction are with three variables: book value to price, 12-month forward earnings to price, and dividend yield. While the current geopolitical shock has dragged the fund off its 52-week high of $80.80, the fund is trading in line with its peers as the Iran conflict roils European and Asian markets. For investors who believe the ceasefire holds, I think AVIV is a buy here.

Fund Breakdown

AVIV was launched by Avantis (part of American Century Investments) with a mandate to achieve broad diversification by investing in a wide set of large-cap companies across non-U.S. developed countries, specifically emphasizing key valuation signals. The fund pursues the same core philosophy Avantis applies across all its offerings—the benefits of indexing (diversification, low turnover, transparency) combined with daily oversight to tilt toward securities with higher expected returns using information in current prices. As noted in the fund's fact sheet, the portfolio management team is led by Eduardo Repetto, Ph.D., with support from Ted Randall, Daniel Ong, CFA, Mitchell Firestein, and Matthew Dubin—they are all veterans from Dimensional Fund Advisors, which gives me reasonable confidence in the pedigree of the process.

The expense ratio of 0.25% is solid for an active fund. The closest passive alternative—the iShares MSCI EAFE Value ETF (EFV) and iShares MSCI Intl Value Factor ETF (IVLU)—charges 0.30% for strictly passive exposure to a similar universe. You're getting active management, a broad portfolio of 584 holdings, and a clean value tilt for less. That's not nothing. From a liquidity standpoint, the fund trades ~89,000 shares per day on average, worth over $6MM—decent for a retail investor, though not deep enough for large institutional block trades. The fund has done well since the start of the year, and with rumors of a ceasefire holding, the fund has started to climb back.

The War Trade and Why It's a Buying Opportunity

Since the U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran intensified in early March 2026 and the Strait of Hormuz was effectively shut to tanker traffic, international equity funds like AVIV have been punished. The fund's mandate is entirely outside the U.S., with heavy exposure to developed markets in Europe and Japan—economies deeply reliant on Gulf oil flows to power their industrial bases. But with the ceasefire announcement, the case to buy AVIV at these levels is compelling. European and Asian equities have been materially oversold on energy fears, and a reopening of Hormuz should unlock a meaningful re-rating across the industrial and energy-heavy developed market names that make up the bulk of this portfolio.

Fund Portfolio and Risk Profile

The nuance that I think the market has overlooked is that AVIV's sector composition actually sets it up well even if elevated oil prices linger for another quarter. Looking at the portfolio, AVIV is significantly overweight energy at 15% versus the benchmark's 5.7%. The fund is pretty similar elsewhere and has taken a point or two off the other sectors to hedge with oil stocks, which are certain to continue to do well even with a temporary ceasefire. The strait won't just reopen to 150 tankers a day like in February, and oil should remain above $80 for the foreseeable future. Otherwise, I like this value setup.

Risks

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 0.93 on a 5-year monthly basis, meaning it tracks the broader market a bit lower from a volatility standpoint. That said, with only about 4.5 years of live data since inception, I'd interpret this number with some caution—the fund has lived through a single rate cycle and a modest amount of geopolitical stress, and the true volatility profile over a full market cycle remains untested. What I can say is that the portfolio's tilt toward cash-flow-generative industrial and energy names, with a value screen applied on top, gives the fund a qualitatively defensive underpinning versus a growth-heavy benchmark. The profits to book of 0.41 as of the recent fund facts signals a solid entry point.

The key downside scenario here is a ceasefire that doesn't hold. If the Iran conflict re-escalates and the Hormuz chokepoint remains disrupted, European and Asian industrial stocks will continue to underperform, and AVIV's recovery thesis will stall. I'd also keep an eye on the CUSMA negotiations in July—Canada is a material part of the portfolio, and any re-escalation in U.S.-Canada trade tensions would add another layer of headwind on top of the Iran situation.

Bottom Line

AVIV is a well-constructed, actively managed international large-cap value ETF that has earned its 0.25% fee by tracking its benchmark (and beating it recently) closely while holding a differentiated portfolio skewed toward industrial, materials, and energy names that have genuine near-term tailwinds if the ceasefire holds. AVIV makes a coherent case—it's a focused value tilt on developed markets, run by a capable team, and available for less than the cost of a passive EAFE value alternative.

The war-driven sell-off has brought the fund down into the $70s and created what I think is an attractive entry point. I rate AVIV a buy on the ceasefire, with the clear caveat that this is not the fund to hold if the conflict re-escalates. For investors with a 12-to-18-month horizon who believe the worst of the Iran shock is behind us, AVIV is one of the more decent risk-reward ETFs in the international equity space right now.

data.title: Foundayo Explained: Lilly's New Weight Loss Pill And The Amazon Effect
data.description:
Thesis

The FDA has just approved Eli Lilly’s (LLY) latest GLP-1 therapy, Foundayo. It’s tipped to come with a number of advantages to compete on, including price, ease of use, and accessibility, as I'll explain. So, not just innovation alone. Foundayo here would reinforce my buy-case thesis in that it meaningfully lowers barriers to entry via oral dosing, simplified administration, competitive pricing, and it is now set to scale distribution via Amazon (AMZN). I think the Amazon factor is going to be a big deal moving forward, and to me, this is how Lilly will widen their addressable market. It's more about moving into a much larger, more convenience-driven population here, which is where the real volume upside is. In my last coverage, I went over Lilly's latest financials and how they came in such contrast to the competition, and now with the latest commercial moves for Foundayo, I think we could see that gap widening.

Foundayo, how it works

As you know, Lilly has just announced that their newly approved drug Foundayo/orforglipron is available for sale in the U.S. Now, I see this as a very big shift for the weight loss market for a number of different reasons. Unlike the earlier GLP-1–based treatments, Foundayo is a once-daily oral pill. It was approved by the U.S. FDA on April 1st of this year, for adults with obesity, or those who are overweight with weight-related conditions. As per the directions, it is to be used alongside diet and exercise. It can be taken at any time throughout the day, with or without food or water, but the tablets must be swallowed whole. So as far as therapies go, this couldn’t be easier for the patient.

If we look into the specifics, there are a number of available strengths ranging from 0.8 mg up to about 17.2 mg. That’s to allow for gradual dose escalation depending on tolerability and response. That range is simply there as a dose-escalation strategy, which is pretty standard for GLP-1–based therapies. We typically see that patients get started on a lower dose at about 0.8 mg or 2.5 mg, and then they gradually increase over weeks or months. This should help the body adapt to the drug’s effects, especially the strain on the gastrointestinal system, to reduce the likelihood of side effects.

Mechanistically speaking, orforglipron is again a small-molecule, non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist. It mimics the action of the natural incretin hormone GLP-1 by enhancing a lot of satiety signals in the brain. This slows gastric emptying and improves metabolic regulation. The main goal here is to reduce calorie intake and lose weight. We saw previously that in Phase 3 trials such as the ATTAIN program, patients on the highest dose lost about 12.4% of body weight, or about 27 pounds, over a 72-week period. There were also improvements in cardiovascular risk markers like blood pressure and lipids, which was good to see.

Differentiation among GLP-1s

Now, every time there is a new weight loss pill on the market or in the works, I always ask what truly makes this one different. I see the weight loss industry as one of the fastest-moving markets, simply because the money and demand are there. Players are always trying new things to stay ahead of the game, and because there are so many ways to compete in this market, be it side effects, overall weight loss, price or ease of use, it leads to interesting dynamics. Each player really focuses on what they’re good at, and for Lilly and Foundayo, it’s not just that it’s oral, but rather how convenient it is compared to other oral GLP-1 attempts.

We know that earlier oral GLP-1 drugs, like oral semaglutide, require a pretty strict fasting and timing rules. However, Foundayo can be taken without any food or water restrictions. It’s a major adherence barrier. Let's take a quick example here with Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) Rybelsus. A patient, a lot of the time it will be someone with type 2 diabetes or obesity, has to take it first thing in the morning on an empty stomach, with only a small sip of water, really no more than about 120 mL. They then have to wait at least 30 minutes before eating, drinking anything else, or, importantly, taking any other medications. Now, that might sound manageable on paper, but in practice, it’s quite restrictive, especially for the very population most likely to use it here. Those people with obesity/diabetes are already struggling with structured eating patterns. They usually wake up hungry, want coffee immediately, or need to take multiple morning medications, which is very common in diabetes.

Pricing

Now, it is still very early days, and pricing can, of course, change and will most likely differ from patient to patient, but from an access and pricing standpoint, Lilly is clearly setting up Foundayo here for broader uptake. What they want is accessibility. They want people not taking a weight loss pill, who have heard about them and are considering taking them, to have the easiest path possible to starting. That also comes with discretion, in that a large portion of those on GLP-1s don’t actually tell others they are on a plan. Now, in the previous days when injectables were costing in the hundreds per month, this was rather difficult. Many needed payer support, which then created a barrier to use in that they would have to go through the clinical process.

As for Foundayo, we’ve heard from Lilly that some insured patients may pay as little as $25/month, whilst self-pay pricing starts at about $149/month. The big thing here is that Lilly wants to undercut many injectable competitors that often exceed $1,000/month without coverage. Another way to ease the pathway into taking GLP-1s is to offer home delivery. This is a bigger factor than most think. Again, it allows for discretion, which many patients would prefer, which is why it is being distributed through LillyDirect with home delivery. But as I said, big pharma GLP-1 manufacturers have lots of ways to compete and will stick to what they’re good at. However, distribution and customer access have always been a challenge, which is why we’re seeing so many pharma companies turn to telehealth providers in recent times. Telehealth companies have a massive advantage simply from data accumulation. If we take the most predominant one recently in Hims & Hers (HIMS), their subscriber base is extremely valuable. They already know who would be most open to a weight loss pill based on customer data, and those already on one treatment subscription are far more open to taking a second. Again, this comes down to ease of access. This is why we saw such a big reaction to Novo re-signing that partnership with Hims; it’s a huge access advantage that big pharma wants.

Ease of access: the Amazon factor

So Novo has Hims, and now we’ve just heard that Lilly is set to offer Foundayo via Amazon. I see this as almost as big as a telehealth partnership. You see, the Amazon partnership is really about removing as much friction as they can from every step of the patient journey; they’re not just lowering prices here. The only advantage I see a telehealth company having over this is a more concentrated customer base. If we think of traditional pathways, getting a GLP-1 medication would involve quite a few steps. You’d have to book a doctor’s appointment, get a prescription, then find a pharmacy that has the drug in stock, which used to be a real issue with GLP-1 shortages. You’d wait in line and sometimes deal with insurance approvals.

What Amazon is essentially doing for Lilly here is compressing that entire chain into something a lot closer to a consumer e-commerce experience. The customer base they’re accessing here is any average person with an Amazon account, which is massive. I see the process working as something like this. You would have to get a prescription; this is, of course, still required and essential. A patient either visits their own doctor or uses a telehealth service, and keep in mind this could be Amazon’s One Medical, if they’re enrolled. So I expect Amazon to update the One Medical portal to have more prominent Foundayo advertising in the near term.

As for prescription routing, instead of sending the prescription to a local pharmacy, it’s sent directly to Amazon Pharmacy. You would then check out like a normal purchase on your Amazon account, see the medication, confirm insurance if applicable or pay the cash price. As for delivery, a lot of the time the medication can arrive the same day, similar to ordering your household items. In some One Medical clinics, I know that patients can even pick it up immediately with the in-office kiosks right after their appointment. It’s a major shift, and I see it as Lilly wanting to turn the medical process into something that feels more like ordering groceries.

Valuation risk

As for Lilly’s valuation, I think it would be reflective of a company which the market sees as a long-duration growth story. If we look at the headline multiples, the stock screens as pretty expensive. It trades at around 27x to 28x forward earnings and about 10.5x forward sales, both of which are well above healthcare sector medians. The premium would look even more pronounced on an enterprise basis, with EV to EBITDA sitting at about 22x forward, nearly double the sector norm. This tells me investors are not really valuing Lilly on current earnings alone here, but more so on the expected expansion of its obesity and diabetes franchise, particularly with drugs like tirzepatide and now Foundayo. Essentially, I see the market pricing in sustained high revenue growth, some strong pricing power and long-term dominance in the GLP-1 category with the current developments. I see this reshaping the company’s revenue mix over the next decade.

Now, that growth expectation is reflected more favourably in the PEG ratio here, which adjusts valuation for earnings expansion. Lilly’s forward PEG is about 1.2x and trailing PEG at 0.4x, which would imply that, relative to their projected EPS growth of about 40% in 2026 and tapering thereafter, the stock is not as stretched as those raw price-to-earnings multiples would imply. In other words, I feel Lilly looks expensive in absolute terms, but appears more reasonably valued on a growth-adjusted basis if we assume those growth forecasts materialise. The key risk in this valuation is execution. That current price would suggest GLP-1 adoption is going to continue scaling globally, with improving access and new formulations to expand the addressable market. Any slowdown in uptake going forward could challenge that premium.

Looking ahead

So just to sum up, Foundayo, for me, would represent more than just another entrant in the GLP-1 space here. It rather signals a shift toward mainstream, scalable obesity treatment. Lilly wants to combine oral convenience, flexible dosing, competitive pricing, and an increasingly frictionless process via common platforms like Amazon. What they’re doing is addressing the biggest barriers that we’ve seen historically limiting uptake. Yes, execution risks remain, and the valuation is already a big chunk of high expectations, but the broader direction is clear: the market is moving toward a much simpler, more accessible therapy plan. And Lilly is positioning itself at the centre of that transition.
